regions.name: Puglia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Late spring (May–June) and early autumn (September–October) offer warm weather, fewer crowds, and ideal conditions for sightseeing and beach visits.

regions.name: Sardinia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: May to June and September to October offer warm weather, fewer crowds, and lower prices than the peak July-August summer season.

regions.name: Sicily
regions.bestTimeToVisit: The best time to visit Sicily is in spring (April–June) or early autumn (September–October) when the weather is warm but not overwhelming and crowds are manageable.

regions.name: Trentino-Alto Adige
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from June to September for hiking and festivals, or December to March for world-class skiing.

regions.name: Tuscany
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to June and September to October offer ideal weather, fewer crowds, and the stunning colors of the Tuscan countryside.

regions.name: Umbria
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to June and September to October offer mild weather, fewer crowds, and vibrant local festivals.

regions.name: Valle d'Aosta
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in winter (December–March) for skiing or summer (June–September) for hiking and festivals.

regions.name: Veneto
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to June or September to October offer mild weather, fewer crowds, and ideal conditions for sightseeing across the region.

top10.name: Rome
top10.description: The Eternal City is Italy's capital and one of the world's great historic destinations, layering ancient ruins, Renaissance art, and vibrant modern life. Every corner reveals millennia of history.

top10.name: Vatican City
top10.description: The independent city-state within Rome that serves as the spiritual and administrative center of the Roman Catholic Church, featuring St. Peter's Basilica and the Vatican Museums. It attracts pilgrims and art lovers from around the world to see masterpieces like Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling.

top10.name: Venice
top10.description: Built on 118 islands connected by canals, Venice is one of the world's most unique and iconic cities. It is renowned for its Gothic architecture, art, and centuries of maritime history.

top10.name: Florence
top10.description: The cradle of the Renaissance, Florence is packed with world-class art, stunning architecture, and vibrant street life. It is the capital of Tuscany and one of Italy's most visited cities.

top10.name: Milan
top10.description: Italy's fashion and financial capital, Milan dazzles with world-class art, Gothic architecture, and a buzzing food scene. It is home to Leonardo da Vinci's Last Supper and the iconic Duomo cathedral.

top10.name: Amalfi Coast
top10.description: The Amalfi Coast is a UNESCO World Heritage stretch of dramatic cliffs, pastel-colored villages, and crystal-clear turquoise waters. Towns like Positano, Ravello, and Amalfi itself are among Italy's most iconic destinations.

top10.name: Pompeii
top10.description: Pompeii is an extraordinarily preserved ancient Roman city buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It offers an unparalleled glimpse into everyday Roman life frozen in time.

top10.name: Naples
top10.description: Naples is the vibrant capital of Campania, known for its rich history, world-famous cuisine, and chaotic energy. The historic center is a UNESCO World Heritage Site filled with churches, underground ruins, and street markets.

top10.name: Cinque Terre
top10.description: Cinque Terre is a UNESCO-listed string of five colorful clifftop villages along a dramatic stretch of Ligurian coastline. The terraced vineyards and pastel-painted houses make it one of Italy's most iconic destinations.

top10.name: Palermo
top10.description: Sicily's vibrant capital blends Arab-Norman architecture with bustling street markets and dynamic street food culture. Palermo is one of Italy's most historically layered and culturally rich cities.

places-index.json.name: Gran Sasso and Monti della Laga National Park
places-index.json.nameIt: Parco Nazionale Gran Sasso e Monti della Laga

taormina.credit: Photo by Federico Di Dio photography (unsplash.com/@didiofederico_photographer)

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 75 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: Carnival of Venice
festivals.description: One of the world's most famous carnivals, famous for its elaborate masks and costumes. The festival fills the streets, canals and squares of Venice with colour and spectacle.

festivals.name: Carnevale di Viareggio
festivals.description: The largest carnival in Italy, famous for its massive satirical floats depicting politicians and public figures. A must-see event on the Tuscan coast.

festivals.name: Ivrea Carnival — Battle of the Oranges
festivals.description: A unique battle where thousands of participants on foot and on carts pelt each other with oranges — re-enacting a medieval revolt against a tyrannical lord.

festivals.name: Mamoiada Mamuthones
festivals.description: Ancient pre-Christian masked ritual where men dressed in sheepskins and heavy cowbells parade through the village in eerie procession. One of Europe's oldest carnival traditions.

festivals.name: Sagra del Mandorlo in Fiore
festivals.description: Festival of the Almond Blossom held in the Valley of the Temples at Agrigento. Folk groups from around the world perform among the ancient Greek ruins.

festivals.name: Epiphany — La Befana
festivals.description: The Feast of the Epiphany marks the end of Christmas celebrations. Children receive gifts (or coal) from La Befana. Major processions in Rome and many towns.

festivals.name: Sant'Agata Festival
festivals.description: One of the largest religious festivals in the world, honouring Catania's patron saint. Over a million people line the streets for the candlelit procession.

festivals.name: Infiorata di Noto
festivals.description: The streets of Noto are carpeted with millions of flower petals arranged in elaborate artistic patterns. One of the most visually stunning events in Italy.

festivals.name: Scoppio del Carro — Easter
festivals.description: On Easter Sunday in Florence, a mechanical dove flies from the Duomo along a wire to ignite a cart packed with fireworks outside — a tradition dating to the First Crusade.

festivals.name: Liberation Day
festivals.description: National holiday marking Italy's liberation from Nazi occupation in 1945. Parades, ceremonies and events across Italy, especially in northern cities.

festivals.name: May Day — Festa dei Lavoratori
festivals.description: Labour Day is a national holiday with concerts and events. The free May Day concert in Rome's Piazza San Giovanni is one of Europe's largest open-air music events.

festivals.name: Palio di Siena (July)
festivals.description: The most dramatic horse race in the world — ten horses representing Siena's medieval districts (contrade) thunder around the Piazza del Campo. Intense civic passion and centuries of rivalry.

festivals.name: Palio di Siena (August)
festivals.description: The second Palio of the year, held on the day after the Feast of the Assumption. Equally contested and passionate as the July race.

festivals.name: Feast of San Giovanni
festivals.description: Florence's patron saint day features a historic football game (Calcio Storico) in 16th-century costume and a spectacular fireworks display over the Arno.

festivals.name: Umbria Jazz Festival
festivals.description: One of Europe's finest jazz festivals, held over ten days in Perugia's historic centre. World-class artists perform in piazzas, theatres and outdoor arenas.

festivals.name: Ferragosto
festivals.description: The biggest national holiday of the Italian summer. Most businesses close, Italians head to the sea or mountains, and fireworks light up the night in every town.

festivals.name: Venice Film Festival
festivals.description: The world's oldest film festival, held on the Lido island. Stars, directors, and cinema lovers descend on Venice for screenings, red carpets, and the Golden Lion awards.

festivals.name: Feast of San Gennaro
festivals.description: Naples' most important religious event. Crowds pack the cathedral hoping to witness the liquefaction of the dried blood of the city's patron saint — seen as a sign of good fortune.

festivals.name: White Truffle Fair — Alba
festivals.description: The world's premier truffle fair, held throughout October in Alba. Tastings, auctions, medieval pageants and a donkey race fill the town as the prized white truffle season peaks.

festivals.name: Eurochocolate
festivals.description: Europe's largest chocolate festival transforms Perugia's historic centre into a vast outdoor chocolate market and exhibition over ten days.

festivals.name: Republic Day
festivals.description: National holiday celebrating Italy's 1946 referendum that established the Republic. Military parade on Via dei Fori Imperiali in Rome. Free access to state museums.

festivals.name: Giostra del Saracino
festivals.description: Medieval jousting tournament in Arezzo where costumed knights on horseback charge at a mechanical Saracen figure. Held twice yearly in the main piazza.

festivals.name: Regatta Storica
festivals.description: Historic gondola and rowing regatta on the Grand Canal. Opens with a spectacular procession of historical boats in period costume representing Venice's maritime past.

festivals.name: Salone del Gusto — Slow Food
festivals.description: Biennial world food fair in Turin celebrating artisan producers, traditional recipes, and sustainable food. Thousands of exhibitors from over 100 countries.

festivals.name: Luminara di San Ranieri
festivals.description: On the eve of Pisa's patron saint day, 70,000 candles placed in windows and along the Arno bathe the city in golden light for one magical evening.

festivals.name: Festa della Madonna Bruna
festivals.description: Matera's most important festival. A cart bearing a painting of the Madonna is ceremonially destroyed by the crowd in a spectacular tradition blending pagan and Christian elements.

festivals.name: Tarantella Festival — La Notte della Taranta
festivals.description: Huge open-air folk music festival culminating in a concert with thousands of musicians performing the hypnotic tarantella dance music of the Salento region.

festivals.name: Sagra del Pesce
festivals.description: The Fish Festival in Camogli fries fish in the world's largest pan and distributes it free to thousands of visitors — a beloved Ligurian coastal tradition since 1952.

festivals.name: Calcio Storico Fiorentino
festivals.description: A brutal 16th-century football game played in full historical costume in Piazza Santa Croce. Four historic Florentine neighbourhoods compete in this violent but spectacular spectacle.

festivals.name: Corpus Christi Infiorata
festivals.description: Streets in Spello and other Umbrian towns are carpeted with intricately designed flower petal pictures for the Corpus Christi procession.

festivals.name: Mille Miglia
festivals.description: The world's most beautiful car race — a thousand vintage cars drive from Brescia to Rome and back through the Italian countryside over four days.

festivals.name: Operaestate Festival
festivals.description: Summer festival of performing arts in Bassano del Grappa with opera, theatre, contemporary dance and music in outdoor venues.

festivals.name: Feast of the Assumption
festivals.description: National holiday and major religious feast day. Fireworks and processions throughout Italy. Many towns hold their local summer festival around this date.

festivals.name: All Saints Day
festivals.description: National holiday. Italians visit cemeteries to honour the dead with flowers and candles. A solemn but important cultural tradition across all regions.

festivals.name: Immaculate Conception
festivals.description: National holiday marking the start of the Christmas season. The Pope places flowers at the statue of the Virgin on Piazza di Spagna in Rome.

festivals.name: Christmas Day
festivals.description: Family celebration with elaborate lunches. Midnight Mass is central to Italian Christmas. Nativity scenes (presepi) are displayed in churches and homes across Italy.

festivals.name: New Year's Eve — Capodanno
festivals.description: Italians celebrate with fireworks, concerts in main squares, lentils for luck, and the tradition of throwing old objects out of windows at midnight. Rome's Circo Massimo hosts a major concert.

festivals.name: Barcolana Regatta
festivals.description: The world's largest sailing race by number of participants — over 2,000 boats of all sizes race in the Gulf of Trieste on the second Sunday of October.

festivals.name: Fiera del Tartufo Bianco — San Miniato
festivals.description: Three-weekend truffle fair in the hilltop town of San Miniato celebrating the prized white truffle of the San Miniato hills with tastings and markets.

festivals.name: Carnival of Acireale
festivals.description: One of Sicily's most spectacular carnivals, known for its enormous illuminated floats of allegorical figures and satirical papier-mâché constructions.

festivals.name: Settimana Santa — Holy Week
festivals.description: Holy Week processions throughout southern Italy are particularly intense and atmospheric. In Procida, the Procession of the Mysteries carries elaborate floats through narrow streets on Good Friday.

festivals.name: Giro d'Italia
festivals.description: One of cycling's three Grand Tours passes through spectacular Italian landscapes. Finish line crowds in Milan's Piazza del Duomo provide a memorable finale.

festivals.name: Spoleto Festival dei Due Mondi
festivals.description: Prestigious international arts festival in Spoleto with opera, theatre, dance, and contemporary art exhibitions across the medieval town.

festivals.name: Sagra di Sant'Efisio
festivals.description: Sardinia's most important religious procession — thousands in traditional costume escort the statue of the island's patron saint from Cagliari to Nora and back over four days.

festivals.name: Palio dell'Assunta — Siena
festivals.description: The second Siena Palio of the year, run on 16 August. Preceded by pageantry and historic ceremonies across the city's 17 contrade.

festivals.name: Mortadella please! Festival
festivals.description: Festival dedicated entirely to mortadella — Bologna's iconic cured meat — with tastings, cooking demonstrations and events across the city.

festivals.name: Opera at Arena di Verona
festivals.description: The Roman amphitheatre in Verona hosts spectacular open-air opera performances from June to September. Aida and Nabucco are perennial favourites. One of the great operatic experiences in the world.

festivals.name: Torneo della Quintana
festivals.description: Medieval jousting tournament in Ascoli Piceno with contestants in 15th-century costume competing for the golden arrow. One of Italy's finest medieval re-enactments.

festivals.name: Grape Harvest Festivals — Chianti
festivals.description: Throughout September, Chianti wine villages hold harvest festivals with grape pressing, wine tastings, local food and traditional music. Greve in Chianti holds the largest wine fair.

festivals.name: Todi Festival
festivals.description: International arts and theatre festival held in the medieval hilltop town of Todi, with performances in historic venues across the town.

festivals.name: Sagra delle Sagre
festivals.description: Festival of festivals in Foggia province celebrating traditional Puglian food with hundreds of local producers showcasing olive oil, pasta, cheese, and wine.

festivals.name: RomaEuropa Festival
festivals.description: International contemporary performing arts festival in Rome with theatre, dance, and music from leading European and international companies.

festivals.name: Luminarie di Santa Lucia
festivals.description: Syracuse's patron saint day is celebrated with spectacular illuminations throughout the city and a candlelit procession carrying the silver statue of Santa Lucia.

festivals.name: Befana Regatta
festivals.description: Gondoliers dress as the Befana witch and race down the Grand Canal on Epiphany morning — a quirky and beloved Venetian tradition.

festivals.name: Fiera di Sant'Orso
festivals.description: Centuries-old craft fair in Aosta celebrating traditional Alpine woodcarving, lacework, and ironwork. One of the oldest fairs in Italy, dating to the early Middle Ages.

festivals.name: Processione dei Misteri
festivals.description: Good Friday procession in Trapani carrying 20 life-size sculptural groups depicting the Passion of Christ through the city streets for 24 hours. Deeply moving.

festivals.name: Festa della Rificolona
festivals.description: Florentines parade through the city on the eve of the Madonna's birthday carrying paper lanterns (rificolone) of elaborate designs, a tradition dating to the 17th century.

festivals.name: Pistoia Blues Festival
festivals.description: One of Europe's longest-running blues festivals, held in Pistoia's central piazza with international headliners and emerging artists.

festivals.name: Sagra del Cinghiale
festivals.description: Wild boar festival in the Maremma and Casentino areas celebrating the autumn hunting season with wild boar stews, ragù tastings and traditional Tuscan fare.

festivals.name: Carnevale di Putignano
festivals.description: The longest carnival in the world — running from 26 December to Shrove Tuesday — famous for its satirical papier-mâché floats. One of Italy's oldest carnivals.

festivals.name: Festival della Valle d'Itria
festivals.description: Prestigious opera festival in the trulli district of the Itria Valley, staging rare and forgotten operatic works in atmospheric outdoor settings.

festivals.name: Sagra del Peperoncino
festivals.description: Chilli pepper festival in Diamante celebrating Calabria's fiery food culture with cook-offs, tastings, and the crowning of the hottest pepper.

festivals.name: Natale di Roma
festivals.description: Rome celebrates its legendary founding on 21 April 753 BC with gladiator re-enactments, historical parades, and fireworks. Free admission to the Colosseum and forums.

festivals.name: Torggelen — Grape must season
festivals.description: Traditional South Tyrolean harvest celebration where locals hike between farmhouses (Buschenschanken) sampling new wine, roast chestnuts, and homemade speck.

festivals.name: Christmas Markets — Alto Adige
festivals.description: South Tyrol's Christmas markets are among the most beautiful in Europe, running from late November to Epiphany. Bolzano, Merano, Bressanone and Trento all have exceptional markets.

festivals.name: Dolomiti Baladin Beer Festival
festivals.description: Craft beer festival in the Dolomites celebrating Italian artisan brewing with tastings and mountain scenery.

festivals.name: Festa di San Costantino
festivals.description: Arbëreshë Albanian cultural festival in Brancaleone celebrating the living Albanian heritage of Basilicata's mountain communities with traditional dress, music and food.

festivals.name: Sagra della Porchetta
festivals.description: Festival celebrating Ariccia's famous porchetta — whole roast pig flavoured with rosemary and garlic — the quintessential Roman street food. Enormous quantities consumed.

festivals.name: Regata delle Antiche Repubbliche Marinare
festivals.description: Rotating annual regatta between the four historic maritime republics — Venice, Genoa, Pisa, and Amalfi — with crews in historical costume competing on traditional boats.

festivals.name: Sagra dell'Uva — Marino
festivals.description: The fountains of Marino in the Castelli Romani run with wine instead of water on the first Sunday of October, celebrating the grape harvest. Free wine for all.

festivals.name: Feast of Santa Rosalia
festivals.description: Palermo's patron saint is honoured with a spectacular illuminated float procession through the city centre and fireworks over the sea. One of the grandest festivals in the Mediterranean.

festivals.name: Sagra del Tartufo Nero — Norcia
festivals.description: Festival celebrating the prized Norcia black truffle with tastings, truffle-hunting demonstrations, and local food pairings in the medieval hilltop town.

festivals.name: Carnevale di Cento
festivals.description: One of Italy's most spectacular carnivals, twinned with Rio de Janeiro. Massive floats, costumed processions and battles where spectators throw sweets and confetti.

festivals.name: Ravenna Festival
festivals.description: International classical music and opera festival held in Ravenna's extraordinary Byzantine churches and mosaic-covered basilicas — an unparalleled acoustic and visual experience.

festivals.name: Fiera di San Gennaro
festivals.description: Street fair in Naples surrounding the feast day celebrations for San Gennaro, with markets, food stalls and religious processions in the Spanish Quarter.

items.label: Prehistoric & Italic Tribes
items.summary: Cave dwellers, Bronze Age villages, and warring Italic tribes settle the peninsula.
items.text: Italy's first inhabitants arrived after the last Ice Age. By 1800 BC, the Bronze Age Terramare culture built pile-dwellings in the Po Valley. Successive waves of Indo-European migrants — Latins, Sabines, Oscans, Umbrians — settled different regions. The Etruscans rose to dominance in central Italy, developing a sophisticated urban civilisation with a writing system and advanced metallurgy that would profoundly shape later Roman culture.

facts.event: First post-Ice Age settlers arrive
Neolithic farmers bring agriculture to the peninsula
Bronze Age Terramare culture in Po Valley
Etruscans emerge in Tuscany and Lazio
Greek colonists found cities in southern Italy (Magna Graecia)
Etruscan twelve-city league dominates central Italy
Traditional founding date of Rome by Romulus

items.label: The Roman Kingdom
items.summary: Seven kings rule Rome from the Palatine Hill — legend and history intertwined.
items.text: Rome began as a small Latin settlement on the Palatine Hill, gradually absorbing neighbouring communities. Seven kings ruled in succession, expanding Roman territory and establishing key institutions: the Senate, the legal calendar, and the Capitoline Temple. The last king, the tyrannical Tarquinius Superbus, was expelled in 509 BC, ending the monarchy and giving birth to the Republic.

facts.event: Romulus founds Rome, becomes first king
Numa Pompilius establishes religious institutions
Tullus Hostilius destroys rival Alba Longa
Tarquinius Priscus, first Etruscan king, lays out the Forum
Servius Tullius builds city walls
Tarquinius Superbus expelled; Republic proclaimed

items.label: The Roman Republic
items.summary: From city-state to Mediterranean superpower — through wars, civil strife and genius.
items.text: The Republic developed the constitutional framework that shaped Western governance for millennia: two elected consuls, the Senate, and codified law (the Twelve Tables, 450 BC). Rome shattered Carthage in three Punic Wars (264–146 BC). But success bred inequality and civil war. Sulla, Pompey, Caesar and Octavian fought for dominance, with Julius Caesar's assassination in 44 BC triggering the final collapse of Republican government.

facts.event: Republic founded; first consuls elected
Twelve Tables — Rome's first written legal code
Gauls sack Rome under Brennus
Three Punic Wars; Hannibal crosses the Alps (218 BC)
Spartacus leads a great slave revolt
Caesar crosses the Rubicon, igniting civil war
Julius Caesar assassinated on the Ides of March
Battle of Actium — Octavian defeats Antony and Cleopatra

items.label: The Roman Empire
items.summary: At its peak, Rome ruled 50 million people from Scotland to Mesopotamia.
items.text: Augustus became the first Emperor, ushering in the Pax Romana — two centuries of relative peace. The Empire built 80,000 km of roads, the Colosseum, the Pantheon, and aqueducts supplying millions with fresh water. Constantine legalised Christianity (313 AD) and founded Constantinople (330 AD). The Western Empire fragmented under Germanic invasions, with the last Emperor deposed in 476 AD.

facts.event: Augustus becomes first Emperor
Vesuvius erupts, burying Pompeii
Colosseum completed under Emperor Titus
Empire reaches its greatest extent under Trajan
Edict of Milan — Constantine legalises Christianity
Constantine founds Constantinople as the new capital
Visigoths under Alaric sack Rome
Romulus Augustulus deposed; Western Empire ends

items.label: The Middle Ages
items.summary: Barbarian kingdoms, papal power and the birth of Italian city-states.
items.text: After Rome's fall, Italy fragmented between Germanic kingdoms, the Byzantine Empire, and an increasingly powerful Papacy. Charlemagne's coronation in Rome on Christmas Day 800 established the Holy Roman Empire. Maritime city-states — Venice, Genoa, Pisa, Amalfi — grew fabulously wealthy through Mediterranean trade. The Normans created a multicultural kingdom in Sicily blending Arab, Byzantine and Latin cultures.

facts.event: Fall of Western Roman Empire
Lombards invade northern Italy
Charlemagne crowned Holy Roman Emperor in Rome
Normans take Bari, ending Byzantine rule in the south
Pope Urban II launches the First Crusade
Roger II crowned King of Sicily
Fourth Crusade — Venice profits from sack of Constantinople

items.label: The Renaissance
items.summary: Florence, Venice and Rome relight the torch of Western civilisation.
items.text: The Renaissance ('rebirth') began in the wealthy Italian city-states, where the Medici family of Florence funded an explosion of art and philosophy. Dante wrote the Divine Comedy; Brunelleschi invented perspective; Leonardo da Vinci embodied the polymath ideal; Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel and sculpted David. Italy's political fragmentation left it vulnerable — France and Spain competed for control from 1494.

facts.event: Dante completes the Divine Comedy
Black Death kills perhaps a third of Italy's people
Brunelleschi completes Florence's cathedral dome
Leonardo da Vinci born
France invades Italy — Italian Wars begin
Leonardo paints the Mona Lisa
Michelangelo paints the Sistine Chapel ceiling
Mutinous imperial troops sack Rome

items.label: Foreign Domination
items.summary: Spain then Austria control much of Italy — Baroque art flourishes, politics stagnates.
items.text: Following the Italian Wars, Spain dominated the peninsula — controlling Naples, Sicily, Sardinia and Milan. Galileo was tried by the Inquisition in Rome in 1633. Yet this era produced extraordinary Baroque art: Caravaggio revolutionised painting with dramatic chiaroscuro; Bernini transformed Rome, designing the colonnade of St Peter's Square. After the War of Spanish Succession (1701–14), Austria replaced Spain as the dominant northern power.

facts.event: Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis — Spain dominates Italy
Galileo tried by the Inquisition
Masaniello's revolt against Spanish rule in Naples
Plague kills half of Naples' population
Austria replaces Spain in northern Italy
Medici dynasty ends in Florence
Excavations begin at buried Pompeii

items.label: Napoleon & the Risorgimento
items.summary: Napoleon awakens Italian nationalism; Garibaldi fights for unification.
items.text: Napoleon's invasion in 1796 spread the ideals of liberty and nationalism. After his defeat, the Congress of Vienna (1815) restored Austrian dominance, but the Risorgimento movement grew. Three figures dominated: Mazzini (the prophet), Cavour (the diplomat), and Garibaldi (the soldier). Garibaldi's 'Expedition of the Thousand' conquered the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies in 1860. On 17 March 1861, the Kingdom of Italy was proclaimed.

facts.event: Napoleon invades Italy
Napoleon crowned King of Italy in Milan
Congress of Vienna restores Austrian rule
Year of Revolutions sweeps Italian states
Short-lived Roman Republic crushed by France
Second War of Independence frees Lombardy
Garibaldi's Thousand conquer Sicily and Naples
Kingdom of Italy proclaimed

items.label: Unified Italy
items.summary: A new nation struggles with poverty, emigration and the 'Southern Question'.
items.text: Unification created a nation on paper but enormous challenges remained. Illiteracy stood at 78% in the south and millions emigrated to the Americas. Rome was added in 1870. Italy suffered a humiliating defeat at Adwa (1896) against Ethiopia. The economy began to modernise: railways were built and industries established in Turin and Milan.

facts.event: Venice joins Italy
Rome becomes Italian capital; Pope confined to Vatican
Rome becomes the capital of unified Italy
Italy defeated at Battle of Adwa
Mass emigration — over 4 million Italians leave for Americas
Italy seizes Libya from the Ottoman Empire

items.label: World Wars & Fascism
items.summary: Mussolini's rise, the alliance with Hitler, and Italy's liberation.
items.text: Italy entered WWI on the Allied side (1915) but the peace left Italians feeling cheated — the 'mutilated victory'. This fuelled Mussolini's Fascist movement, which marched on Rome in 1922. He established a dictatorship and allied with Hitler. After Allied landings in Sicily (1943) the King dismissed Mussolini. Italy became a brutal battleground until liberation in 1945.

facts.event: Italy enters WWI on the Allied side
Italian army routed at Caporetto
March on Rome — Mussolini becomes Prime Minister
Lateran Treaty — Vatican becomes independent state
Italy invades Ethiopia
Italy enters WWII alongside Nazi Germany
Allied landing in Sicily; Mussolini arrested
Armistice signed; Germany occupies the north
Mussolini executed by partisans

items.label: The Italian Republic
items.summary: The Economic Miracle transforms Italy from rubble to the world's fifth largest economy.
items.text: Italians voted to abolish the monarchy and establish a Republic in 1946. Italy joined NATO (1949) and was a founding member of the EEC (1957). The 'Economic Miracle' of the 1950s–60s saw GDP grow at 6% a year; millions moved north to work in Fiat and Olivetti factories. The 1970s brought the 'Years of Lead' — political terrorism from the Red Brigades, culminating in the murder of PM Aldo Moro in 1978.

facts.event: Republic proclaimed after referendum
Republican Constitution takes effect
Italy signs Treaty of Rome — founding EEC member
Rome Olympics; Fellini's La Dolce Vita released
Florence flooded — international art rescue effort
Piazza Fontana bombing opens the 'Years of Lead'
Aldo Moro kidnapped and murdered by Red Brigades

items.label: Modern Italy
items.summary: From Tangentopoli to the Euro — Italy's turbulent path in the modern world.
items.text: In 1992–94, 'Tangentopoli' ('Bribesville') — a vast anti-corruption investigation — destroyed the entire postwar political class. Silvio Berlusconi dominated public life for two decades. Italy adopted the Euro in 2002. The 2008 financial crisis led to years of austerity. The COVID-19 pandemic struck Italy first in Europe in early 2020. Despite turbulence, Italy holds more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country.

facts.event: Bologna station bombing kills 85
Tangentopoli investigations collapse the old parties
Italy adopts the Euro
Italy wins the FIFA World Cup
L'Aquila earthquake devastates Abruzzo
Italy first European country devastated by COVID-19
Giorgia Meloni elected — Italy's first female Prime Minister
Italy has 61 UNESCO World Heritage Sites — more than any other country (China is next with 60).

items.label: No cappuccino after 11am
items.text: Italians only drink milky coffee in the morning. Ordering one after lunch marks you as a tourist — not offensive, just unusual.

items.label: Espresso is just 'caffè'
items.text: Ask for 'un caffè' and you get an espresso. It's drunk standing at the bar in 30 seconds.

items.label: Ordering at the bar
items.text: Pay at the cassa (till) first, get a receipt, then take it to the barista and say what you want.

items.label: Caffè corretto
items.text: Espresso with a shot of grappa or sambuca — an Italian morning tradition in some regions.

items.label: Caffè shakerato
items.text: Espresso shaken with ice and sugar — the Italian iced coffee. Order this in summer.

items.label: Prices
items.text: Espresso at the bar: €1–1.50. At a table (servizio): double. Tourist areas: triple.

items.label: Antipasto
items.text: Starter — bruschetta, cured meats, olives, seafood

items.label: Primo
items.text: First course — pasta, risotto, soup. This is often the main event.

items.label: Secondo
items.text: Main course — meat or fish. Usually served alone, no sides included.

items.label: Contorno
items.text: Side dish — order separately with your secondo

items.label: Dolce
items.text: Dessert — tiramisù, panna cotta, gelato, cannoli

items.label: Caffè
items.text: Coffee — always after dessert, never with it

items.label: Coperto
items.text: Cover charge (€1.50–4) per person — listed on the menu. Legal and normal.

items.label: Servizio
items.text: Service charge (10–15%) may be included. Check before adding a tip.

items.label: Tipping
items.text: Not obligatory. Leave €1–2 per person for good service — leave cash on the table.

items.label: Rome
items.text: Cacio e pepe, carbonara, amatriciana — the Roman pasta holy trinity

items.label: Naples
items.text: Pizza Margherita — invented here in 1889. Thin base, San Marzano tomatoes, buffalo mozzarella

items.label: Florence
items.text: Bistecca alla Fiorentina — T-bone steak, grilled rare over charcoal, sold by weight

items.label: Tuscany
items.text: Ribollita — hearty bread and bean soup, the original Tuscan peasant food

items.label: Sicily
items.text: Arancini, pasta alla Norma, fresh cannoli — buy from a pasticceria, not a tourist shop

items.label: Emilia-Romagna
items.text: Parmigiano-Reggiano, prosciutto di Parma, mortadella — the food capital of Italy

items.label: Venice
items.text: Sarde in saor, bacalà mantecato, risotto al nero di seppia (squid ink risotto)

items.label: Calabria
items.text: Nduja (spreadable spicy salami), fileja pasta, peperoncino everything

items.label: Bar
items.text: For coffee and breakfast (cornetto + cappuccino). Open from 7am.

items.label: Trattoria
items.text: Family-run, traditional, affordable. No frills, great food. Go here.

items.label: Osteria
items.text: Even more rustic than a trattoria — wine-focused with simple food.

items.label: Ristorante
items.text: Formal and pricier. Better for special occasions than everyday meals.

items.label: Pizzeria
items.text: Order by the whole pizza — sharing is unusual. Eat at the table, not walking.

items.label: Mercato
items.text: Food markets are the best places to eat cheaply and authentically. Ask locals where theirs is.

items.label: Avoid
items.text: Restaurants with pictures on the menu, touts at the door, or 'tourist menu' signs — all red flags.

items.label: Artisanal gelato
items.text: Look for gelato displayed in metal tubs with lids (pozzetti) — this is the real thing. Avoid gelato piled high in mountainous peaks with bright unnatural colours.

items.label: Gusti
items.text: Choose 1–3 flavours (gusti). Classic: pistachio (pistacchio), hazelnut (nocciola), stracciatella.

items.label: Cone or cup?
items.text: Cono (cone) or coppetta (cup). Ask for panna (whipped cream) on top — often free.

items.label: Price
items.text: €2–4 for a small. Pay first at the till in many shops.

items.label: Gelato vs ice cream
items.text: Lower fat, more intense flavour, served slightly warmer. Nothing like supermarket ice cream.

items.label: Vino della casa
items.text: House wine — order a carafe (una caraffa) or half-carafe. Cheap, honest, often very good.

items.label: Aperitivo
items.text: The pre-dinner drink ritual, 6–8pm. Order a Campari Spritz or Aperol Spritz. In Milan many bars serve free nibbles with your aperitivo.

items.label: Digestivo
items.text: After-dinner drink — Limoncello (south), Grappa (north), Amaro. Usually free at the end of a meal in traditional restaurants.

items.label: Acqua frizzante
items.text: Sparkling water — if you want still water, ask for acqua naturale or acqua liscia.

items.label: Regional wines
items.text: Barolo & Barbaresco (Piedmont), Chianti (Tuscany), Brunello di Montalcino, Amarone (Veneto), Nero d'Avola (Sicily)
Validate your ticket before boarding or risk a €50+ fine — even with a reserved seat.

items.label: Trenitalia
items.text: National rail operator — book at trenitalia.com or Trainline app. Regional trains (Regionale) are cheap; Frecciarossa is the high-speed option between cities.

items.label: Validating
items.text: Insert your paper ticket into the yellow/green stamping machine (obliteratrice) on the platform before boarding. E-tickets don't need validating.

items.label: Classes
items.text: 1st class is worth it on long Frecciarossa routes. Regional trains have no class distinction.

items.label: Strikes (Sciopero)
items.text: Strikes are common. Check trenitalia.com the day before. Core services must run 9–13:00 and 17–21:00 by law.

items.label: Luggage
items.text: No luggage restrictions but overhead racks are small — a 56×45×25cm cabin-sized bag fits most racks.

items.label: Buy before boarding
items.text: Tickets from tabacchi (tobacco shops), newsstands or apps — not usually on the bus. Validate immediately on boarding.

items.label: Rome
items.text: ATAC buses run across the city. Bus 40 and 64 link the train station to the Vatican. Avoid peak hours (8–9am, 6–7pm).

items.label: Milan
items.text: ATM runs an excellent network. Buy 90-minute tickets (€2.20) or a day pass (€7).

items.label: Florence
items.text: ATAC buses and trams. Much of the centre is ZTL (restricted zone) — cars banned, buses and taxis only.

items.label: Naples
items.text: Buses are crowded and slow. The Circumvesuviana train is far better for Pompeii and Sorrento.

items.label: Rome
items.text: Line A (orange) serves Vatican, Spanish Steps, Termini. Line B (blue) serves Colosseum. Single ticket €1.50 — valid 100 minutes.

items.label: Milan
items.text: Best metro in Italy — 5 lines, runs until 12:30am (2am weekends). Integrated with buses and trams.

items.label: Naples
items.text: Limited but useful — Line 1 serves the historic centre, Piazza Garibaldi and the airport.

items.label: No metro
items.text: Florence, Venice and most small cities have no metro. Walking and buses are the way around.
Only use licensed white taxis with a meter — avoid unlicensed drivers at airports and stations.

items.label: Hailing
items.text: Taxis can't be hailed on the street in most cities — find a taxi rank (posteggio) or use the IT Taxi app.

items.label: Fares
items.text: Meter starts at €3–4. Airport surcharges apply. Sunday and night rates (10pm–6am) are higher.

items.label: Tipping
items.text: Not expected but rounding up is appreciated — e.g. pay €20 on a €17.50 fare.

items.label: Apps
items.text: IT Taxi is the official app (works nationwide). Uber operates in Rome and Milan but is pricier.

items.label: What is it?
items.text: The vaporetto is Venice's water bus — the only public transport through the city. Operated by ACTV.

items.label: Tickets
items.text: Single ride €9.50 — expensive. A 24-hour pass (€25) or 48-hour pass (€35) is far better value if you explore.

items.label: Line 1
items.text: Slow boat down the Grand Canal — stops at every landing. Best for sightseeing. Line 2 is express.

items.label: Validate
items.text: Tap your ticket or pass at the yellow reader before stepping onto the pontile (landing stage).

items.label: Water taxis
items.text: Private water taxis are beautiful but cost €80–150 per journey. Split with others to make it worthwhile.
ZTL zones (Zona a Traffico Limitato) are camera-enforced restricted areas in most historic centres — fines arrive months later by post.

items.label: Autostrada
items.text: Toll motorways — pay at booths with cash or card. Fast and well-maintained.

items.label: Driving style
items.text: Italians drive confidently. Lanes are treated as suggestions on country roads. Assertive but calm wins.

items.label: Parking
items.text: Blue lines = paid parking, white = free, yellow = residents only. Parcheggio signs lead to official car parks.

items.label: Speed limits
items.text: 50 km/h in towns, 90 km/h on roads, 110 km/h dual carriageways, 130 km/h autostrada. Strictly enforced by speed cameras.

items.label: Headlights
items.text: Mandatory outside urban areas at all times of day — even in sunshine.
Never use unlicensed taxi touts at airports — they will charge €80–150 for journeys that should cost €25–50. Only use white licensed taxis from the official rank, or pre-book.

items.label: Rome Fiumicino (FCO)
items.text: Leonardo Express train runs every 30 min to Roma Termini — €14, takes 32 min. Much faster and cheaper than a taxi. Also FL1 regional trains to Trastevere, Ostiense and Tiburtina — €8.

items.label: Rome Ciampino (CIA)
items.text: Terravision and SIT buses to Roma Termini — €6, takes 40 min. No train link — bus is the best option.

items.label: Milan Malpensa (MXP)
items.text: Malpensa Express train to Milano Centrale (35 min, €13) or Cadorna (30 min, €13). Malpensa Shuttle bus to Centrale — €10 but slower. Taxi fixed fare: €65 to central Milan.

items.label: Milan Linate (LIN)
items.text: ATM bus 73 to Centrale — €2, takes 25 min. Taxi: €25–30 fixed rate to centre.

items.label: Venice Marco Polo (VCE)
items.text: ACTV water bus (vaporetto) to Venice — €15, scenic, takes 75 min. Alilaguna ferry — similar price. Land taxi to Piazzale Roma — €40, then on foot or water bus. No direct train.

items.label: Florence (FLR)
items.text: Tramvia T2 tram to Santa Maria Novella station — €1.70, takes 20 min. Taxi fixed rate: €22.

items.label: Naples (NAP)
items.text: Alibus to Piazza Garibaldi — €5, takes 30–50 min. Taxi fixed rate: €23 to centre.

items.label: Taxi scam warning
items.text: Always insist the driver uses the meter (tassametro), or agree a fixed price before getting in. Licensed taxis have the city's coat of arms on the door and a meter display.

items.label: Sicily
items.text: Regular ferries from Villa San Giovanni (Calabria) to Messina — 20 min crossing, run by Caronte & Tourist, very frequent. Overnight ferries from Naples to Palermo (10 hrs) and Genoa to Palermo (20 hrs) via Grimaldi and GNV.

items.label: Sardinia
items.text: Ferries from Civitavecchia (near Rome), Genoa, Livorno and Naples. GNV, Tirrenia and Moby Lines operate routes. Crossings take 6–19 hours. Book well ahead in July/August — very busy.

items.label: Amalfi Coast
items.text: Ferry and hydrofoil services connect Amalfi, Positano, Ravello and Salerno from spring to autumn — far more scenic and quicker than the coastal road. ALICOST and TravelMar operate these routes.

items.label: Capri & Ischia
items.text: Hydrofoils (aliscafi) from Naples Beverello pier — 40 min to Capri, 50 min to Ischia. Multiple sailings daily. Slow ferry also available.

items.label: Lake Garda
items.text: Navigarda ferries connect towns including Sirmione, Bardolino, Malcesine and Riva del Garda. Car ferries cross between Torri del Benaco and Maderno. Excellent way to avoid lake road traffic.

items.label: Lake Como
items.text: Regular ferries between Como, Bellagio, Varenna and Menaggio. Car ferries between Bellagio and Varenna. Buy tickets at the dock — no need to book ahead outside peak season.

items.label: Booking tips
items.text: For overnight ferries book 4–6 weeks ahead in summer. A cabin is worth the extra cost for overnight crossings. Bring your own food — on-board restaurants are expensive.

items.label: Roaming (UK)
items.text: Since Brexit, most UK networks charge for EU roaming again. Check your tariff before travel — some (e.g. O2, Three) still include EU data; others charge £2–5/day.

items.label: Buy an Italian SIM
items.text: Cheapest option for longer stays. Available at airports, phone shops and tabacchi. You'll need your passport.

items.label: TIM
items.text: Best rural coverage across Italy, especially in southern Italy and islands. Visitor SIM: ~€15 for 30GB + 200 min.

items.label: Vodafone Italy
items.text: Good coverage, especially in cities and northern Italy. Tourist SIM: ~€15 for 20GB.

items.label: Iliad
items.text: Cheapest option — €9.99/month for 150GB with no contract. Coverage slightly behind TIM/Vodafone in remote areas but excellent in cities.

items.label: WindTre
items.text: Good city coverage, competitive tourist packages. Available at airports.

items.label: eSIM option
items.text: Airalo, Holafly and Ubigi offer Italian eSIMs you can buy before travelling and activate instantly. From around £8 for 5GB. Ideal if your phone supports eSIM.

items.label: Free WiFi
items.text: Most hotels, bars and restaurants offer free WiFi. 'Avete il WiFi?' (Do you have WiFi?) and 'Qual è la password?' (What's the password?) are useful phrases.
